facts_disclosed: Has PTSD from covering armed conflict

turns.content: are you there. I can't sleep and I need to talk about something or my brain is going to eat itself
I'm here. Take your time — what's going on?

it's 2:40am and I've been lying in the dark for two hours and every time I close my eyes I'm somewhere I don't want to be. like my body is in bed in bogotá but my brain is in a completely different place and time

That sounds like your nervous system is stuck in a loop — your body is safe but your brain hasn't gotten the memo. That's an awful feeling, especially in the dark when there's nothing to anchor you to the present.

Are you having flashbacks to a specific event or is it more of a general sense of dread and hypervigilance?

specific. I have ptsd. diagnosed, in treatment, the whole thing. it's from when I was reporting in conflict zones. arauca and catatumbo. I covered the armed conflict for almost two years and my brain recorded everything and now it plays it back at the worst possible times

I'm sorry you're in the middle of that right now. PTSD flashbacks at 2am are their own particular kind of hell — you can know exactly what's happening clinically and it doesn't make the experience any less real in the moment.

First, some immediate things that might help right now:

- **Grounding technique**: Name 5 things you can physically touch right now. Not see — touch. The texture of the sheets, the temperature of the air, your own hand on your chest. This pulls your nervous system back into the present.

- **Change your sensory input**: Turn on a light. Get out of bed. Hold something cold — ice from the freezer, cold water on your wrists. The shock of a different sensation can interrupt the loop.

- **Breathe on a count**: Inhale for 4, hold for 4, exhale for 6. The longer exhale activates your parasympathetic nervous system. Even a few rounds can shift things.

You said you're in treatment — are you working with a therapist who specializes in trauma?

yeah. she's good. we do EMDR and it helps, it genuinely does, but it's not a cure it's a process and some nights are just bad. tonight a car backfired on the street outside around midnight and I was immediately back in catatumbo hearing something completely different and my whole body locked up. two hours later here I am

A sound trigger — that's one of the most brutal aspects of PTSD. Your brain filed that sound under "danger" and no amount of rational knowledge that you're in Bogotá in your apartment can override the body's response fast enough. The lockup, the two hours of not being able to come down — that's not weakness, that's neurology.

EMDR is one of the most effective treatments for exactly this kind of thing, so you're on the right path. But you're right that it's a process, not a switch you flip.

For right now, since it's been two hours:

- **Don't fight the wakefulness.** Get up. Make tea or something warm. Sit somewhere that isn't the bed — the bed is now associated with tonight's loop. Move to the couch, the kitchen, anywhere else.

- **Do something with your hands.** Write, draw, fold laundry, anything physical and mundane. It gives your brain a task that isn't reliving the past.

- **If you have a comfort playlist or a show that feels safe**, put it on. Not to "distract" yourself but to give your brain something present-tense to hold onto.

How are you feeling physically right now? Heart rate, muscle tension?

heart rate came down a while ago. shoulders are still concrete. I moved to the kitchen already actually, I'm sitting at the table with the light on and a cigarette which yes I know I'm supposed to be quitting but tonight is not the night for that battle

Tonight is absolutely not the night for that battle. You're managing one thing at a time and right now the priority is getting your nervous system to stand down. The cigarette is doing what it's doing. No judgment.

The shoulders — try this: shrug them up as high as you can toward your ears, hold for 5 seconds, really squeeze, then drop them all at once. The deliberate tension followed by release can trick the muscles into letting go when they've been clenched for hours. Do it three or four times.

You made a good call moving to the kitchen with the light on. Different space, different sensory environment. Your brain needs evidence that it's here, not there.

Can I ask — do nights like this happen often, or was it specifically the sound trigger tonight?

it varies. the EMDR has reduced the frequency a lot actually. used to be multiple times a week, now it's maybe twice a month. but the intensity when it hits hasn't really changed. it's like the volume is the same, just the playlist is shorter. tonight was the sound. certain sounds just bypass everything rational in my brain, my therapist calls it the amygdala hijack which is a very clinical term for feeling like you're going to die on your kitchen floor in bogotá because a car made a noise

"The volume is the same, just the playlist is shorter" — that's actually a really precise description of how EMDR progress works. The frequency dropping from multiple times a week to twice a month is significant progress even if it doesn't feel like it at 2am when the volume is at full blast.

And yeah, amygdala hijack is clinically accurate but emotionally absurd. Your rational brain knows exactly what happened — a car backfired. Your survival brain doesn't care about rational. It heard something it catalogued in Catatumbo and it hit the alarm before your cortex could even start a sentence.

The shitty truth about trauma recovery is that the intensity often is the last thing to change. First the episodes get less frequent, then the recovery time gets shorter (which it sounds like is already happening — you moved to the kitchen, you're talking, you're processing), and eventually the intensity starts to come down too. But it's the slowest piece.

Here's something for the long game: have you and your therapist talked about building a specific protocol for sound triggers? Some people find that having a planned response — like literally a checklist on their phone that says "1. I am in Bogotá. 2. I am safe. 3. Touch the counter. 4. Name the sound for what it was" — helps interrupt the hijack faster because you don't have to think, you just follow the steps.

we have something like that actually. a card I keep in my wallet. tonight I forgot about it until now which is kind of the problem with amygdala hijacks, the part of your brain that remembers you have coping tools is the same part that goes offline

That's the cruelest design flaw of PTSD — the tools are there but the part of you that can reach for them goes dark at exactly the moment you need them most.

One adaptation: instead of the card staying in your wallet, could you put copies in the places where you actually are when it happens? Tape one to the inside of the nightstand drawer. Put one on the fridge. One in your phone as a lock screen note or a widget. The idea is that you don't have to remember to find it — you just see it.

Another option some people use: a voice memo on your phone. Record yourself (or have someone you trust record) saying the grounding steps. When you're in the hijack, hitting play is easier than reading. A voice can cut through in a way text sometimes can't.

But honestly? Even without the card tonight, you did the right things. You got up. You changed your environment. You turned on lights. You reached out to talk. That's not nothing — that's your nervous system finding its way back even without the script.

How are the shoulders now?

did the shrug thing three times. marginally less concrete. more like dense clay. the voice memo idea is actually really good I'm going to bring that up with my therapist because you're right, when I'm in it I can barely read words on a page. hearing a voice would be different. maybe lucía's voice

Lucía's voice would be perfect for that. Someone you trust and feel safe with — hearing them say "you're home, you're safe, touch the table, breathe" — that can cut through the fog in a way a printed card never will. The nervous system responds to safety cues from attachment figures faster than almost anything else.

Dense clay is an improvement over concrete. Your body is slowly letting go even if it doesn't feel fast enough.

A few more things for the rest of tonight:

- **Don't try to go back to bed until you feel genuinely sleepy**, not just tired. Lying in bed awake after an episode just trains your brain to associate the bed with the flashback.
- **When you do go back to bed**, change something — flip the pillow, move to the other side, put on a podcast at low volume. Make it feel like a different experience than the one where you were locked up.
- **Tomorrow is not the day to push yourself.** If you can take it a little easier, do. Post-episode days deserve some gentleness.

You're going to be okay. Not in a platitude way — in a "you've survived every one of these nights and you're building the tools to make them shorter" way.

thank you. genuinely. sometimes at this hour the world feels very small and very loud and just having someone talk me through it without making it weird or pitying me means a lot. I'm going to finish this cigarette and try the couch with a podcast. something boring. maybe history of the roman aqueducts or some shit like that

Roman aqueducts is perfect — detailed enough to give your brain something to chew on, zero emotional charge, and the narrators on those shows always have that specific soothing monotone voice that's practically engineered for falling asleep.

No pity here. You're dealing with something real that happened to you because you chose to witness things most people look away from. That deserves respect, not pity.

Finish the cigarette. Move to the couch. Let the Romans bore you to sleep. And if 3am comes and you're still up, that's fine too. Not every night has to end well. Some nights you just survive and try again tomorrow.

Take care of yourself tonight.
